1:
"Now I say, That the heir, as long as he is a child, differeth nothing from a servant, though he be lord of all;" The witnesses note the heir in his minority no different from a servant. On "the heir, as long as he is a child," the grammatical voices note the figure. Barnes and Robertson note Paul illustrates from the law of inheritance: an "heir," "as long as he is a child" (a minor, in his nonage), "differeth nothing from a servant" — though destined to own all, in his minority he is under restraint like a slave.

On "though he be lord of all," the witnesses note the dignity in waiting. Barnes and Calvin note that the child, "though lord of all" (the rightful owner of the whole estate), has not yet entered on his inheritance; his ownership is real but not yet possessed.

The lutheran Luther and latin-fathers Tertullian note the application: this pictures the church under the old covenant — heirs of the promise, yet held in the bondage of the law until the time of fulfilment. The verse displays the heir in his minority no different from a servant.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul illustrates from inheritance — an "heir, as long as he is a child (a minor), differeth nothing from a servant," under restraint like a slave though destined to own all (Barnes, Robertson); "though lord of all," the rightful owner, he has not yet entered his inheritance (Barnes, Calvin). Luther and Tertullian note this pictures the church under the old covenant, heirs yet in bondage. The verse displays the heir in minority. The verse marks the heir: a child. The verse displays the likeness: to a servant. The verse displays the ownership: lord of all. The verse displays the application: the old covenant. The figure displays the heir restrained until his time. The verse displays the minority: of the child. The verse displays the bondage: like a servant. The verse displays the inheritance: not yet possessed. The heir in his minority no different from a servant displays Paul's figure for the church under the law: as a child, though rightful owner of all, is held under restraint like a slave until he comes of age, so God's heirs under the old covenant were held in bondage until the time of fulfilment. The verse displays the heir's nonage, kept under tutors and governors (v.2).
2:
"But is under tutors and governors until the time appointed of the father." The brief verse notes the heir under tutors until the appointed time. The grammatical voices note the guardians. Barnes and Robertson note the minor heir "is under tutors (guardians of his person) and governors (stewards of his estate)" — placed under the control and care of others during his nonage, not free to act for himself.

On "until the time appointed of the father," the witnesses note the limit. Barnes and Luther note that this subjection lasts only "until the time appointed of the father" — a fixed term, set by the father, at which the heir comes of age and enters his freedom and inheritance.

The lutheran Luther notes the application: Moses, as tutor and governor, held the church under the law's discipline until the appointed time — the coming of Christ, who ends the time of law and begins the time of grace. The verse displays the heir under tutors until the appointed time.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the minor heir "is under tutors (guardians of his person) and governors (stewards of his estate)," under others' control during his nonage (Barnes, Robertson); this lasting only "until the time appointed of the father," a fixed term set by the father (Barnes, Luther). Luther notes Moses held the church under the law's discipline until Christ's coming. The verse displays the heir under guardians. The verse marks the tutors: guardians and stewards. The verse displays the limit: the appointed time. The verse displays the father: who sets it. The verse displays the application: the law as tutor. The subjection displays the heir governed until his appointed freedom. The verse displays the tutors: over him. The verse displays the time: appointed. The verse displays the father: ordaining. The heir under tutors until the appointed time displays the temporary nature of the law's discipline: as a minor is placed under guardians and stewards only until the father's fixed term, so the church was held under the law until the appointed time — the coming of Christ, who brings the heirs to their freedom. The verse displays the heir's appointed term, applied to the church's bondage (v.3).
3:
"Even so we, when we were children, were in bondage under the elements of the world:" The witnesses note the church in its nonage in bondage under the elements of the world — and the crux of those "elements." On "even so we, when we were children, were in bondage," the grammatical voices note the application. Barnes and Robertson note Paul applies the figure: "even so we (chiefly the Jews, but including all before Christ), when we were children (in our religious nonage), were in bondage" — held in a servile state under the old dispensation.

On "the elements of the world" (stoicheia), the interpreters divide. The grammatical-historical Barnes, Ellicott, and the Reformed Calvin, with Clarke and Wesley, take the "elements (rudiments) of the world" to mean the elementary, rudimentary teaching of religion — the ceremonial law, the ABCs or first principles, earthly figures veiling a heavenly mystery, "of the world" because outward and material. Some older commentators (noted by Ellicott and Robertson) took it as the physical elements of nature or the heavenly bodies (governing festivals or worshipped by the heathen).

The witnesses note the bondage: these rudimentary observances, transient and elementary, held the church in a kind of servitude until Christ came. The verse displays the church in its nonage in bondage under the elements of the world.

The witnesses agree the application: "we (chiefly the Jews), when we were children (in religious nonage), were in bondage" under the old dispensation (Barnes, Robertson). And most take "the elements of the world" as the rudiments or first principles of religion — the ceremonial law, the ABCs, earthly figures veiling a heavenly mystery, "of the world" because outward and material (Calvin, Barnes, Clarke, Wesley, Ellicott). But some older commentators (noted by Ellicott, Robertson) took the stoicheia as the physical elements of nature or the heavenly bodies. The verse displays the bondage under the elements. The verse marks the application: we in our nonage. The verse displays the debate: rudiments or physical elements. The verse displays the bondage: under the law. The verse displays the transience: elementary, passing. The bondage displays the church held under rudimentary observance. The verse displays the children: in nonage. The verse displays the elements: of the world. The verse displays the servitude: until Christ. The church in its nonage in bondage under the elements of the world displays the servile state of the old dispensation: held as children under the rudiments — read by most as the ceremonial law and first principles of religion, by some as the elements of nature — the people were in bondage until Christ came to bring them to maturity and freedom. The verse displays the elementary bondage, ended by the sending of the Son (vv.4-5).
4:
"But when the fulness of the time was come, God sent forth his Son, made of a woman, made under the law," The witnesses note the incarnation in the fulness of time — the pre-existent Son made of a woman, with the crux of the virgin birth. On "the fulness of the time," the grammatical voices note the appointed season. Barnes and Robertson note that "the fulness of the time" was the complete, predetermined season ordained by God — when prophecy converged, the world lay at peace under Rome, and all was ripe for the Redeemer's coming.

On "God sent forth his Son," the witnesses note the pre-existence and deity. Barnes, Calvin, and Robertson note that "God sent forth his Son" implies the Son's pre-existence and deity — he existed with God before he was sent (John 1:1; Philippians 2:6); "his Son" affirms his Godhead.

On "made of a woman," interpreters divide. The Reformed Calvin, with Gill, Clarke, Wesley, and the eastern Cyril, take it to denote the virgin birth — Christ formed of his mother's substance, not by ordinary generation, born of a virgin without a man. The grammatical-historical Ellicott and Robertson hold "born of a woman" is a common idiom (Matthew 11:11) denoting simply Christ's true humanity, with no direct reference to the miraculous conception (though it agrees with it). On "made under the law," the witnesses note the subjection: the Son placed himself under the law's precept and curse, to redeem those under it.

The witnesses agree the great truths: in "the fulness of the time" (the complete, God-ordained season) "God sent forth his Son" — implying his pre-existence and deity (John 1:1) — "made under the law," subject to its precept and curse to redeem those under it (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). But "made of a woman" divides them: Calvin, Gill, Clarke, Wesley, and Cyril take it of the virgin birth (formed of his mother's substance, not ordinary generation); Ellicott and Robertson take it as common idiom for true humanity, no direct reference to the miraculous conception. The verse displays the incarnation in the fulness of time. The verse marks the season: the fulness of time. The verse displays the pre-existence: God sent his Son. The verse displays the debate: virgin birth or true humanity. The verse displays the subjection: under the law. The incarnation displays the pre-existent Son made man under the law. The verse displays the fulness: of time. The verse displays the Son: sent forth. The verse displays the woman: of whom he was made. The incarnation in the fulness of time displays the central mystery: at the God-ordained season the pre-existent Son was sent forth, made of a woman — read as the virgin birth (Calvin, Cyril) or as true humanity (Ellicott, Robertson) — and made under the law, that he might redeem those under it. The verse displays the sending of the Son, with its redeeming purpose (v.5).
5:
"To redeem them that were under the law, that we might receive the adoption of sons." The brief verse notes the twofold purpose: redemption from the law and adoption as sons. The grammatical voices note the first aim. Barnes and Robertson note the first purpose of the incarnation: "to redeem (buy back, ransom) them that were under the law" — to free those held in bondage under the law's precept and curse.

On "that we might receive the adoption of sons," the witnesses note the second aim. Barnes and Calvin note the further purpose: "that we might receive the adoption of sons (huiothesia)" — being redeemed from slavery, believers are taken into God's family as sons, with the full rights of sonship.

The lutheran Luther notes the order: Christ entered the holy place by his own blood and obtained eternal redemption, so that those once slaves under the law become free sons and heirs; redemption issues in adoption. The verse displays the twofold purpose: redemption and adoption.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the first purpose "to redeem (ransom) them that were under the law," freeing those in bondage to its precept and curse (Barnes, Robertson); the second "that we might receive the adoption of sons," believers taken into God's family with the full rights of sonship (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes redemption issues in adoption — slaves become free sons and heirs. The verse displays redemption and adoption. The verse marks the first aim: redeem from the law. The verse displays the second: adoption of sons. The verse displays the freedom: from bondage. The verse displays the family: God's sons. The purpose displays the redeemed taken into God's family as sons. The verse displays the redemption: from the law. The verse displays the adoption: as sons. The verse displays the order: redemption then sonship. The twofold purpose — redemption from the law and adoption as sons — displays the goal of the incarnation: Christ came to ransom those held in bondage under the law and to bring them, so freed, into God's family as adopted sons with all the rights of sonship. The verse displays the adoption of sons, sealed by the Spirit crying Abba (v.6).
6:
"And because ye are sons, God hath sent forth the Spirit of his Son into your hearts, crying, Abba, Father." The witnesses note the Spirit of the Son sent into the heart, crying Abba, Father. On "because ye are sons, God hath sent forth the Spirit of his Son," the grammatical voices note the gift. Barnes and Robertson note that "because ye are sons" (already adopted), God sends "the Spirit of his Son" — the Holy Spirit, the Spirit of Christ — "into your hearts"; the Spirit is the seal and evidence of sonship.

On "the Spirit of his Son," the witnesses note the deity and procession. Barnes and Cyril note that the Spirit is called "the Spirit of his Son" — proceeding from and belonging to the Son as to the Father (Romans 8:9) — the personal, living Spirit who is God; an incidental witness to the Trinity.

On "crying, Abba, Father," the Reformed Calvin and lutheran Luther note the filial cry: the Spirit makes the believer cry "Abba, Father" — the Aramaic and Greek words together expressing the intimate, confident address of a child to his father; both Jew and Gentile cry to God as Father. The verse displays the Spirit of the Son sent into the heart, crying Abba, Father.

The witnesses agree: "because ye are sons" (already adopted), God sends "the Spirit of his Son" — the Holy Spirit, the Spirit of Christ — "into your hearts," the seal and evidence of sonship (Barnes, Robertson); the Spirit called "the Spirit of his Son," proceeding from and belonging to the Son as to the Father (Romans 8:9), a witness to his deity (Barnes, Cyril); and the Spirit makes the believer cry "Abba, Father," the intimate, confident address of a child (Calvin, Luther). The verse displays the Spirit crying Abba. The verse marks the gift: the Spirit of his Son. The verse displays the ground: because ye are sons. The verse displays the deity: the Spirit of the Son. The verse displays the cry: Abba, Father. The gift displays the Spirit witnessing the believer's sonship. The verse displays the Spirit: sent forth. The verse displays the heart: indwelt. The verse displays the cry: filial. The Spirit of the Son sent into the heart, crying Abba, Father, displays the seal of adoption: because believers are already sons, God sends the Spirit of his Son into their hearts, the living witness who makes them cry to God as Father — the intimate, confident address of a child to his father. The verse displays the Spirit of sonship, confirming the believer's heirship (v.7).
7:
"Wherefore thou art no more a servant, but a son; and if a son, then an heir of God through Christ." The brief verse notes the believer no longer a servant but a son and heir. The grammatical voices note the conclusion. Barnes and Robertson note Paul draws the conclusion ("wherefore"): "thou art no more a servant (slave, in bondage under the law) but a son" — the believer's status is changed from slave to son.

On "if a son, then an heir of God through Christ," the witnesses note the inheritance. Barnes and Calvin note the further step: "if a son, then an heir" — sonship carries heirship — "an heir of God" (heir to the inheritance God gives) "through Christ" (by virtue of union with Christ, the great Heir).

The lutheran Luther notes the liberty: the believer, once a slave under the law, is now a free son and heir of all God's blessings; the whole argument of the epistle — freedom in Christ — is sealed here. The verse displays the believer no longer a servant but a son and heir.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul concludes "thou art no more a servant (slave under the law) but a son," the believer's status changed (Barnes, Robertson); "if a son, then an heir of God through Christ," sonship carrying heirship to God's inheritance by union with Christ (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes the believer is now a free son and heir, the epistle's theme of freedom sealed. The verse displays son and heir. The verse marks the change: no more a servant. The verse displays the status: a son. The verse displays the heirship: an heir of God. The verse displays the means: through Christ. The conclusion displays the slave become son and heir of God. The verse displays the servant: no more. The verse displays the son: now. The verse displays the heir: of God. The believer no longer a servant but a son and heir displays the crown of the argument: redeemed and adopted, the believer is no longer a slave under the law but a son — and being a son, an heir of God himself, through union with Christ. The verse displays the believer's liberty and heirship, against the folly of returning to bondage (vv.8-11).
8:
"Howbeit then, when ye knew not God, ye did service unto them which by nature are no gods." The brief verse notes the Galatians' former bondage to false gods in their ignorance. The grammatical voices note their past. Barnes and Robertson note Paul recalls the Galatians' former state: "when ye knew not God" (in their heathen ignorance), "ye did service (were in bondage, served as slaves) unto them which by nature are no gods" — the idols they once worshipped.

On "by nature are no gods," the witnesses note the falsehood. Barnes and Henry note the idols were "by nature no gods" — they had no real divinity, being only the work of men's hands or the creatures of imagination; the Galatians had served what was nothing.

The puritan Henry notes the contrast: Paul reminds them of their former slavery to false gods in order to expose the folly of returning, now that they know the true God, to any form of bondage. The verse displays the Galatians' former bondage to false gods in their ignorance.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul recalls the Galatians' former state — "when ye knew not God" (in heathen ignorance), "ye did service (were in bondage) unto them which by nature are no gods," the idols they worshipped (Barnes, Robertson); the idols having no real divinity, the work of men's hands, so they served what was nothing (Barnes, Henry). Henry notes Paul recalls this to expose the folly of returning to bondage. The verse displays their former bondage. The verse marks the ignorance: knew not God. The verse displays the service: to no gods. The verse displays the falsehood: idols nothing. The verse displays the contrast: now they know God. The bondage displays the Galatians once enslaved to false gods. The verse displays the ignorance: of God. The verse displays the idols: no gods. The verse displays the slavery: to nothing. The Galatians' former bondage to false gods in their ignorance displays the depth from which they were saved: before they knew God, they were enslaved to idols that were by nature no gods at all — a slavery to nothing, which makes their drift back toward bondage the more inexcusable now that they know the true God. The verse displays their heathen past, set against the folly of turning back (v.9).
9:
"But now, after that ye have known God, or rather are known of God, how turn ye again to the weak and beggarly elements, whereunto ye desire again to be in bondage?" The witnesses note the rebuke for turning back to the weak and beggarly elements. On "now, after ye have known God, or rather are known of God," the grammatical voices note the correction. Barnes and Robertson note Paul says they now "know God," but corrects himself — "or rather are known of God" — for their knowing him springs from his first knowing (choosing, owning) them; grace is the source.

On "how turn ye again to the weak and beggarly elements," the witnesses note the folly. Barnes and Calvin note the astonished rebuke: "how turn ye again to the weak and beggarly elements (the same stoicheia, the rudiments)" — the ceremonial observances, called "weak" (powerless to justify or save) and "beggarly" (poor, bringing no spiritual riches).

On "ye desire again to be in bondage," the lutheran Luther notes the bondage: to take up the law's ceremonies as necessary to righteousness is to return to slavery — exchanging the liberty of Christ for the yoke they had escaped. The verse displays the rebuke for turning back to the weak and beggarly elements.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul says they now "know God" but corrects himself — "or rather are known of God" — their knowing springing from his first knowing (choosing, owning) them, grace the source (Barnes, Robertson); the astonished rebuke "how turn ye again to the weak (powerless to save) and beggarly (poor) elements," the ceremonial rudiments (Barnes, Calvin); to take them up as necessary to righteousness being a return to slavery (Luther). The verse displays the rebuke. The verse marks the correction: known of God. The verse displays the folly: turning to weak elements. The verse displays the description: weak and beggarly. The verse displays the bondage: desired again. The rebuke displays the Galatians forsaking liberty for poverty. The verse displays the knowing: of God. The verse displays the elements: weak. The verse displays the bondage: resumed. The rebuke for turning back to the weak and beggarly elements displays Paul's astonishment: having come to know God — or rather to be known of him by grace — the Galatians were turning again to the powerless, poverty-stricken rudiments of ceremony, desiring once more the very bondage from which Christ had freed them. The verse displays the folly of relapse, instanced in their observance of days (v.10).
10:
"Ye observe days, and months, and times, and years." The witnesses note the Galatians' observance of the Jewish calendar — with the question of which observance is condemned. On the catalogue, the grammatical voices note the festivals. Barnes and Robertson note Paul instances their Judaizing: "ye observe (scrupulously watch) days (sabbaths and fast-days), and months (new moons), and times (the seasonal feasts — Passover, Pentecost, Tabernacles), and years (the sabbatical year and jubilee)" — taking on the whole Jewish ceremonial calendar.

On what is condemned, interpreters carefully qualify. The Reformed Calvin, with Wesley, Robertson, and the lutheran Luther, hold that Paul condemns not the civil reckoning of time, nor free and orderly Christian observance, but the observance that binds the conscience — that treats days as holy in themselves and necessary to righteousness (the Judaizers' demand); Robertson notes Paul kept these as a Jew but objected to Gentiles taking them up as a means of salvation. The grammatical-historical Barnes extends the warning further — against all days not commanded in Scripture, including saints' days and even Protestant festivals — while Ellicott (citing Origen) holds Christian seasons are not necessarily condemned but belong to the "elements."

The witnesses note the danger: to make calendar observance a matter of righteousness is to fall back into the bondage of the law. The verse displays the Galatians' observance of the Jewish calendar.

The witnesses agree the instance: Paul names their Judaizing observance — "days (sabbaths, fasts), months (new moons), times (the feasts), years (sabbatical year, jubilee)" — the whole ceremonial calendar (Barnes, Robertson). But what is condemned divides them: Calvin, Wesley, Robertson, and Luther hold it is only the observance that binds conscience and is made necessary to righteousness, not civil reckoning or free Christian order (Paul kept these as a Jew); Barnes extends the warning to all non-commanded days, including saints' days and Protestant festivals; Ellicott (with Origen) holds Christian seasons not necessarily condemned but "elements." The verse displays the calendar observance. The verse marks the catalogue: days, months, times, years. The verse displays the debate: which observance is condemned. The verse displays the danger: righteousness by calendar. The verse displays the bondage: relapse to law. The observance displays the Galatians returning to the ceremonial calendar. The verse displays the days: observed. The verse displays the feasts: kept. The verse displays the bondage: resumed. The Galatians' observance of the Jewish calendar displays the mark of their relapse: they had taken up days, months, seasons, and years — the whole ceremonial calendar — and the voices agree that to make such observance a matter of righteousness (Calvin, Luther), though they differ on whether all Christian holy days fall under the warning (Barnes vs Calvin), is to fall back into the bondage of the law. The verse displays the sign of their bondage, over which Paul fears (v.11).
11:
"I am afraid of you, lest I have bestowed upon you labour in vain." The brief verse notes Paul's fear that his labour among them was in vain. The grammatical voices note the anxiety. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's tender fear: "I am afraid of (for) you, lest I have bestowed upon you labour in vain" — he dreads that all his toil in planting the gospel among them may prove fruitless if they fall back into bondage.

On "labour in vain," the witnesses note the concern. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's solicitude: if they abandon the gospel of grace for the law, his ministry among them would have come to nothing; their relapse would frustrate his labour.

The lutheran Luther notes the gravity: the danger is real, for to seek righteousness by the law is to fall from grace; Paul's fear shows how serious their drift was. The verse displays Paul's fear that his labour was in vain.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's tender fear — "I am afraid of (for) you, lest I have bestowed upon you labour in vain," dreading that his toil may prove fruitless if they fall back (Barnes, Robertson); if they abandon grace for the law, his ministry would come to nothing (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes the danger is real, for to seek righteousness by law is to fall from grace. The verse displays Paul's fear. The verse marks the fear: afraid for you. The verse displays the concern: labour in vain. The verse displays the danger: relapse to law. The verse displays the gravity: falling from grace. The fear displays Paul dreading the loss of his work. The verse displays the anxiety: for them. The verse displays the labour: at risk. The verse displays the relapse: feared. Paul's fear that his labour among them was in vain displays the depth of his pastoral anxiety: he dreads that all his toil in planting the gospel of grace may prove fruitless, should the Galatians fall back into the bondage of the law and so frustrate his ministry. The verse displays the apostle's solicitude, softened into a personal appeal (vv.12-20).
12:
"Brethren, I beseech you, be as I am; for I am as ye are: ye have not injured me at all." The brief verse notes Paul's affectionate appeal to be as he is. The grammatical voices note the entreaty. Barnes and Robertson note Paul turns from argument to tender appeal: "brethren, I beseech you, be as I am" — be free from the law, as I am — "for I am as ye are" (I became as a Gentile, free from the law, for your sake, 1 Corinthians 9:21); he asks them to follow his example of liberty.

On "ye have not injured me at all," the witnesses note the reassurance. Barnes and Henry note Paul adds "ye have not injured me at all" — he bears them no personal grievance; his appeal springs not from any wrong done to him but from love and concern for them.

The lutheran Luther notes the tenderness: after sharp rebuke, Paul softens, calling them "brethren" and pleading with affection; the pastor mixes severity with love. The verse displays Paul's affectionate appeal to be as he is.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul turns to tender appeal — "be as I am (free from the law), for I am as ye are (I became as a Gentile for your sake)," asking them to follow his example of liberty (Barnes, Robertson); adding "ye have not injured me at all," bearing them no grievance, his appeal springing from love not personal wrong (Barnes, Henry). Luther notes Paul softens after rebuke, pleading with affection. The verse displays the affectionate appeal. The verse marks the entreaty: be as I am. The verse displays the example: I am as ye are. The verse displays the reassurance: no injury. The verse displays the tenderness: brethren. The appeal displays Paul pleading from love for their liberty. The verse displays the beseeching: of Paul. The verse displays the freedom: his example. The verse displays the love: his motive. Paul's affectionate appeal to be as he is displays the turn from argument to entreaty: calling them brethren, he pleads that they share his liberty from the law, as he became as one of them for their sake — and assures them his appeal springs not from any personal grievance but from love. The verse displays the apostle's tender plea, recalling their first warm reception (vv.13-15).
13:
"Ye know how through infirmity of the flesh I preached the gospel unto you at the first." The brief verse notes Paul first preaching to them amid bodily infirmity. The grammatical voices note the circumstance. Barnes and Robertson note Paul recalls that "through infirmity (weakness, sickness) of the flesh I preached the gospel unto you at the first" — it was some bodily ailment that was the occasion (or attended his first preaching) among the Galatians.

On "infirmity of the flesh," the witnesses note the affliction. Barnes and Calvin note that Paul labored among them under a real physical weakness or illness — perhaps the same "thorn in the flesh" (2 Corinthians 12:7); his outward condition was unimpressive, even repellent.

The lutheran Luther notes the contrast: despite Paul's weak and afflicted appearance, the Galatians had received his gospel gladly; their first love stands in sharp contrast to their present coldness. The verse displays Paul first preaching to them amid bodily infirmity.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul recalls that "through infirmity (weakness, sickness) of the flesh I preached the gospel unto you at the first," some bodily ailment attending his first preaching (Barnes, Robertson); he labored under a real physical weakness, perhaps the thorn in the flesh, his appearance unimpressive (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes that despite his weak appearance the Galatians received him gladly. The verse displays preaching amid infirmity. The verse marks the circumstance: infirmity of the flesh. The verse displays the occasion: first preaching. The verse displays the affliction: bodily weakness. The verse displays the contrast: gladly received then. The circumstance displays Paul preaching despite bodily weakness. The verse displays the infirmity: of the flesh. The verse displays the gospel: preached. The verse displays the first time: recalled. Paul first preaching to them amid bodily infirmity displays the humble circumstance of their conversion: it was under some real physical weakness or illness that he first brought them the gospel — and despite his unimpressive, afflicted appearance, they received him gladly, a memory Paul recalls to shame their present coldness. The verse displays the apostle's weak estate, met by their warm reception (vv.14-15).
14:
"And my temptation which was in my flesh ye despised not, nor rejected; but received me as an angel of God, even as Christ Jesus." The brief verse notes the Galatians receiving Paul as an angel of God despite his affliction. The grammatical voices note the reception. Barnes and Robertson note that Paul's "temptation (trial) which was in my flesh" (his bodily affliction, a trial to him and a test to them) they "despised not, nor rejected (loathed, spurned)" — his infirmity did not make them turn from him.

On "received me as an angel of God, even as Christ Jesus," the witnesses note the honour. Barnes and Calvin note the height of their welcome: they received Paul "as an angel (messenger) of God" — even "as Christ Jesus" himself — with the highest reverence and love, as though Christ spoke in him.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the contrast: such was their first devotion that they honoured the afflicted apostle as if he were Christ — which makes their present readiness to forsake him the more grievous. The verse displays the Galatians receiving Paul as an angel of God.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's "temptation (trial) in my flesh" (his bodily affliction) they "despised not, nor rejected (loathed)," his infirmity not turning them from him (Barnes, Robertson); they received him "as an angel of God, even as Christ Jesus," with the highest reverence, as though Christ spoke in him (Barnes, Calvin). Wesley notes such first devotion makes their present coldness the more grievous. The verse displays receiving Paul as an angel. The verse marks the trial: in his flesh. The verse displays the reception: not despised. The verse displays the honour: as an angel, as Christ. The verse displays the contrast: first devotion. The reception displays the Galatians honouring the afflicted apostle. The verse displays the temptation: not rejected. The verse displays the welcome: as Christ. The verse displays the reverence: highest. The Galatians receiving Paul as an angel of God despite his affliction displays the warmth of their first love: far from spurning his bodily trial, they welcomed him with the highest reverence — as a messenger of God, even as Christ Jesus himself — a devotion that makes their present drift the more painful. The verse displays their former honour, raising the question of their lost blessedness (v.15).
15:
"Where is then the blessedness ye spake of? for I bear you record, that, if it had been possible, ye would have plucked out your own eyes, and have given them to me." The brief verse notes Paul recalling their former blessedness and self-giving love. The grammatical voices note the lost joy. Barnes and Robertson note Paul asks "where is then the blessedness (the happiness, the felicitation) ye spake of?" — the joy and self-congratulation they once felt at having received the gospel; where had it gone?

On "ye would have plucked out your own eyes, and given them to me," the witnesses note their devotion. Barnes and Calvin note the proverbial expression of their love: "if it had been possible, ye would have plucked out your own eyes (your most precious members) and given them to me" — they would have parted with anything, even their eyes, for Paul's sake.

The lutheran Luther notes the pathos: Paul recalls their once-boundless love to expose, by contrast, the coldness into which the false teachers had drawn them. The verse displays Paul recalling their former blessedness and self-giving love.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul asks "where is then the blessedness ye spake of?" — the joy they once felt at receiving the gospel, now vanished (Barnes, Robertson); their proverbial devotion, that "ye would have plucked out your own eyes and given them to me," parting with anything for Paul's sake (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes Paul recalls their once-boundless love to expose their present coldness. The verse displays their former love. The verse marks the loss: where is the blessedness? The verse displays the devotion: their own eyes. The verse displays the self-giving: anything for Paul. The verse displays the contrast: lost joy. The recollection displays the Galatians' once-boundless love. The verse displays the blessedness: faded. The verse displays the eyes: offered. The verse displays the coldness: now. Paul recalling their former blessedness and self-giving love displays the painful contrast: he asks where the joy has gone that they once felt at receiving the gospel — a love so great they would have plucked out their own eyes for him — exposing how far the false teachers had drawn them from their first devotion. The verse displays the lost first love, which made truth-telling seem like enmity (v.16).
16:
"Am I therefore become your enemy, because I tell you the truth?" The brief verse notes Paul asking if telling the truth made him their enemy. The grammatical voices note the poignant question. Barnes and Robertson note Paul asks, with grief, "am I therefore become your enemy, because I tell you the truth?" — has his faithful telling of the truth (in this very letter, and before) turned their love into hostility?

On the irony, the witnesses note the reversal. Barnes and Calvin note the painful irony: those who once loved him as Christ now regard him as an enemy — and for no other cause than that he speaks the truth to them; faithfulness has been requited with coldness.

The lutheran Luther notes the common lot: it is the perpetual experience of faithful ministers that telling unwelcome truth makes them seem enemies; flatterers are loved, truth-tellers resented. The verse displays Paul asking if telling the truth made him their enemy.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul asks with grief "am I therefore become your enemy, because I tell you the truth?" — has his faithful truth-telling turned their love to hostility? (Barnes, Robertson); the painful irony that those who loved him as Christ now count him an enemy, for no cause but that he speaks truth (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes it is the common lot of faithful ministers that unwelcome truth makes them seem enemies. The verse displays the poignant question. The verse marks the question: become your enemy? The verse displays the cause: telling the truth. The verse displays the irony: love turned to hostility. The verse displays the lot: of faithful ministers. The question displays Paul grieved that truth made him an enemy. The verse displays the enemy: he seems. The verse displays the truth: he tells. The verse displays the coldness: requiting faithfulness. Paul asking if telling the truth made him their enemy displays the grief of the faithful pastor: those who once loved him as Christ now seem to count him an enemy — and for no other cause than that he speaks the truth to them, faithfulness requited with hostility. The verse displays the apostle's pained question, exposing the false teachers' motives (v.17).
17:
"They zealously affect you, but not well; yea, they would exclude you, that ye might affect them." The brief verse notes the false teachers' self-serving zeal for the Galatians. The grammatical voices note the courtship. Barnes and Robertson note Paul exposes the Judaizers: "they zealously affect you (court you, make much of you, pay you eager attention) but not well (not honourably, with no good motive)" — their zeal is a flattering courtship with a selfish end.

On "they would exclude you, that ye might affect them," the witnesses note the design. Barnes and Calvin note the false teachers' real aim: "they would exclude you (shut you out — from Paul, from gospel liberty, from the wider church)" so "that ye might affect (court, be devoted to) them" — to make the Galatians dependent on themselves alone.

The puritan Henry notes the contrast with Paul: the false teachers sought to monopolize the Galatians' devotion for their own glory, whereas Paul sought their good; selfish ambition is the mark of the false. The verse displays the false teachers' self-serving zeal for the Galatians.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul exposes the Judaizers — "they zealously affect you (court you eagerly) but not well (with no good motive)," a flattering courtship with a selfish end (Barnes, Robertson); their real aim "to exclude you (shut you out from Paul and gospel liberty) that ye might affect (be devoted to) them," making the Galatians dependent on themselves (Barnes, Calvin). Henry notes they sought to monopolize devotion for their own glory. The verse displays their self-serving zeal. The verse marks the courtship: they affect you. The verse displays the motive: not well. The verse displays the design: exclude you. The verse displays the end: that ye affect them. The zeal displays the false teachers courting the Galatians selfishly. The verse displays the affecting: of them. The verse displays the exclusion: intended. The verse displays the devotion: sought for themselves. The false teachers' self-serving zeal for the Galatians displays the mark of the deceivers: they courted the Galatians eagerly but with no honest motive, seeking to shut them off from Paul and gospel liberty so that the Galatians would be devoted to them alone — selfish ambition masked as zeal. The verse displays the deceivers' design, against which good zeal is commended (v.18).
18:
"But it is good to be zealously affected always in a good thing, and not only when I am present with you." The brief verse notes that zeal is good when it is in a good cause and constant. The grammatical voices note the principle. Barnes and Robertson note Paul grants that zeal itself is not bad: "it is good to be zealously affected (to be the object of earnest attention, or to be earnest) always in a good thing" — zeal is commendable when its object is good.

On "not only when I am present," the witnesses note the constancy. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's wish that their zeal for the good be constant — "not only when I am present with you" but always; he would have their devotion to the truth steady, not dependent on his presence.

The grammatical-historical note the contrast: unlike the false teachers' selfish and fitful courtship, true zeal is directed to a good end and endures whether the apostle is present or absent. The verse displays that zeal is good when in a good cause and constant.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul grants zeal itself is not bad — "it is good to be zealously affected always in a good thing," commendable when its object is good (Barnes, Robertson); his wish that their zeal be constant, "not only when I am present," steady and not dependent on his presence (Barnes, Calvin). The grammatical-historical note the contrast with the false teachers' fitful, selfish courtship. The verse displays good and constant zeal. The verse marks the principle: zeal in a good thing. The verse displays the constancy: always. The verse displays the wish: not only when present. The verse displays the contrast: with selfish zeal. The principle displays zeal commended when good and steady. The verse displays the zeal: good. The verse displays the cause: good. The verse displays the constancy: always. That zeal is good when it is in a good cause and constant displays Paul's balanced judgment: he does not condemn earnest zeal itself, but commends it when its object is good and its devotion steady — unlike the false teachers' selfish and fitful courtship, true zeal endures whether the apostle is present or absent. The verse displays the mark of right zeal, voiced in Paul's travail for them (v.19).
19:
"My little children, of whom I travail in birth again until Christ be formed in you," The witnesses note Paul travailing in birth again until Christ be formed in them. On "my little children," the grammatical voices note the tenderness. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's deeply affectionate address: "my little children (teknia)" — a term of the tenderest love, as a parent to little ones; he is their spiritual father, yearning over them.

On "of whom I travail in birth again," the witnesses note the pain. Barnes and Calvin note the striking figure: Paul is "in travail (the pangs of childbirth) again" for them — as if their relapse had undone his earlier labour, so that he must endure the pains of bringing them to spiritual birth a second time.

On "until Christ be formed in you," the lutheran Luther and puritan Henry note the goal: "until Christ be formed in you" — until Christ's image, his life and likeness, is fully shaped within them; the aim of all his travail is their conformity to Christ. The verse displays Paul travailing in birth again until Christ be formed in them.

The witnesses agree: Paul's tender address "my little children (teknia)," as a parent to little ones, yearning over them as their spiritual father (Barnes, Robertson); the striking figure that he is "in travail (childbirth pangs) again," their relapse having undone his earlier labour so he must bring them to birth a second time (Barnes, Calvin); "until Christ be formed in you" — until Christ's image and likeness is fully shaped within them, the goal of all his travail (Luther, Henry). The verse displays Paul's travail. The verse marks the address: my little children. The verse displays the pain: travail again. The verse displays the goal: Christ formed in them. The verse displays the love: a father's. The travail displays Paul's parental pain for their conformity to Christ. The verse displays the children: his own. The verse displays the birth-pangs: renewed. The verse displays the Christ: to be formed. Paul travailing in birth again until Christ be formed in them displays the depth of his parental love: addressing them as his little children, he likens his anguish over their relapse to the pangs of childbirth endured a second time — labouring until the very image of Christ is fully shaped within them. The verse displays the apostle's spiritual travail, longing to be present and change his tone (v.20).
20:
"I desire to be present with you now, and to change my voice; for I stand in doubt of you." The brief verse notes Paul's wish to be present and change his tone, perplexed over them. The grammatical voices note the longing. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's wish: "I desire to be present with you now, and to change my voice" — he longs to be with them in person, so as to vary his tone (to speak more tenderly, or to adapt his words to their state, as a letter cannot).

On "I stand in doubt of you," the witnesses note the perplexity. Barnes and Calvin note Paul "stands in doubt (is perplexed, at a loss) of (about) you" — he is uncertain how to deal with them, what to think of their state, torn between hope and fear; he does not know whether to be severe or gentle.

The lutheran Luther notes the pastoral difficulty: a faithful minister, perplexed by a wavering people, longs to be present that he may read their hearts and suit his words; the cold medium of a letter cannot do what a living voice can. The verse displays Paul's wish to be present and change his tone, perplexed over them.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's wish "to be present with you now, and to change my voice," longing to be with them so as to vary his tone (speak more tenderly, or adapt to their state) as a letter cannot (Barnes, Robertson); he "stands in doubt (is perplexed, at a loss) of you," uncertain how to deal with them, torn between hope and fear (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes the perplexed minister longs to read their hearts in person. The verse displays Paul perplexed. The verse marks the wish: to be present. The verse displays the desire: change my voice. The verse displays the perplexity: doubt of you. The verse displays the difficulty: severe or gentle. The wish displays Paul longing to be present and suit his words. The verse displays the presence: desired. The verse displays the voice: to change. The verse displays the doubt: of them. Paul's wish to be present and change his tone, perplexed over them, displays his pastoral distress: he longs to be with them in person so as to soften or vary his words as a letter cannot, for he stands at a loss about their state — torn between hope and fear, unsure whether to deal sharply or gently. The verse displays the apostle's perplexity, turning to the law's own witness (vv.21-31).
21:
"Tell me, ye that desire to be under the law, do ye not hear the law?" The brief verse notes Paul challenging the legalists from the law itself. The grammatical voices note the challenge. Barnes and Robertson note Paul turns to argue from Scripture: "tell me, ye that desire to be under the law" (who wish to be subject to the Mosaic law) "do ye not hear (heed, understand) the law?" — do they not listen to what the law itself teaches?

On "the law," the witnesses note the double sense. Barnes and Henry note that "the law" here means the Pentateuch, the books of Moses — the very Scriptures the legalists revere; Paul will draw from them (the history of Abraham's sons) an argument against the legalists' own position.

The puritan Henry notes the strategy: Paul meets the Judaizers on their own ground, appealing to the law they prize to show that it testifies for grace and freedom, not bondage. The verse displays Paul challenging the legalists from the law itself.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul turns to argue from Scripture — "tell me, ye that desire to be under the law, do ye not hear (heed) the law?" — do they not listen to what the law teaches? (Barnes, Robertson); "the law" here meaning the Pentateuch, the books of Moses they revere, from which Paul will argue against them (Barnes, Henry). Henry notes Paul meets the Judaizers on their own ground. The verse displays the challenge from the law. The verse marks the challenge: do ye not hear the law? The verse displays the audience: those under the law. The verse displays the sense: the Pentateuch. The verse displays the strategy: their own ground. The challenge displays Paul arguing from the law the legalists prize. The verse displays the hearing: of the law. The verse displays the desire: to be under it. The verse displays the appeal: to Moses. Paul challenging the legalists from the law itself displays his strategy: he meets those who wish to be under the law on their own ground, asking whether they truly hear what the law — the books of Moses they revere — actually teaches, for he will draw from it an argument for grace and freedom against them. The verse displays the appeal to the law, opening the allegory of Abraham's sons (vv.22-31).
22:
"For it is written, that Abraham had two sons, the one by a bondmaid, the other by a freewoman." The brief verse notes the two sons of Abraham, by the bondmaid and the freewoman. The grammatical voices note the history. Barnes and Robertson note Paul cites the record (Genesis): "Abraham had two sons" — Ishmael, "by a bondmaid (Hagar, the slave)," and Isaac, "by a freewoman (Sarah, the free wife)" — the historical fact on which the allegory will rest.

On the two mothers, the witnesses note the contrast. Barnes and Calvin note the foundational distinction: one son was born of a slave, the other of the free wife — a difference of mothers that Paul will use to picture the difference between the two covenants.

The lutheran Luther notes the figure: Abraham, with his two sons by two women, pictures God with two kinds of people — the children of bondage and the children of freedom; the history is the seed of the allegory. The verse displays the two sons of Abraham, by the bondmaid and the freewoman.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul cites the record — "Abraham had two sons," Ishmael "by a bondmaid (Hagar, the slave)" and Isaac "by a freewoman (Sarah)," the historical fact for the allegory (Barnes, Robertson); the foundational distinction of mothers — one a slave, the other free — picturing the two covenants (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes Abraham's two sons picture two kinds of people. The verse displays the two sons. The verse marks the record: Abraham's two sons. The verse displays the mothers: bondmaid and freewoman. The verse displays the contrast: slave and free. The verse displays the figure: seed of the allegory. The history displays the two sons by two mothers. The verse displays Ishmael: of the bondmaid. The verse displays Isaac: of the freewoman. The verse displays the distinction: slave and free. The two sons of Abraham, by the bondmaid and the freewoman, display the historical ground of Paul's argument: Abraham had two sons — one born of Hagar the slave, the other of Sarah the free wife — a difference of mothers on which Paul will build his picture of the two covenants. The verse displays the foundational history, marked by the manner of each birth (v.23).
23:
"But he who was of the bondwoman was born after the flesh; but he of the freewoman was by promise." The brief verse notes the contrast between the son born after the flesh and the son of promise. The grammatical voices note the manner of birth. Barnes and Robertson note the key difference: Ishmael, "of the bondwoman, was born after the flesh" — by ordinary, natural generation, the result of Abraham and Hagar's own contrivance; while Isaac, "of the freewoman, was by promise" — born through God's special promise, beyond nature (Sarah being past age).

On "after the flesh... by promise," the witnesses note the symbolism. Barnes and Calvin note the contrast that grounds the allegory: "after the flesh" (the way of nature and human effort) against "by promise" (the way of grace and divine power) — Ishmael representing what man produces, Isaac what God gives.

The lutheran Luther notes the gospel point: Isaac, the child of promise, pictures believers born not by the works of the law (the flesh) but by the promise of grace; the manner of birth is the heart of the allegory. The verse displays the contrast between the son born after the flesh and the son of promise.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Ishmael "of the bondwoman, was born after the flesh" (by natural generation, human contrivance), while Isaac "of the freewoman, was by promise" (through God's special promise, beyond nature, Barnes, Robertson); the contrast "after the flesh" (nature and human effort) against "by promise" (grace and divine power), grounding the allegory (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes Isaac pictures believers born by the promise of grace, not the works of the law. The verse displays flesh and promise. The verse marks the bondwoman's son: after the flesh. The verse displays the freewoman's son: by promise. The verse displays the contrast: nature and grace. The verse displays the gospel: born by promise. The contrast displays Ishmael of the flesh, Isaac of promise. The verse displays the flesh: human effort. The verse displays the promise: divine grace. The verse displays the births: opposed. The contrast between the son born after the flesh and the son of promise displays the heart of the allegory: Ishmael came by natural generation and human contrivance, Isaac by God's promise beyond nature — the one picturing what man produces by the flesh, the other what God gives by grace. The verse displays the flesh-and-promise contrast, interpreted as the two covenants (vv.24-26).
24:
"Which things are an allegory: for these are the two covenants; the one from the mount Sinai, which gendereth to bondage, which is Agar." The witnesses note the allegory of the two covenants — with the question of allegory versus type. On "which things are an allegory," the interpreters carefully define the method. The grammatical-historical Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson, with Clarke and the Reformed Calvin, insist that Paul does not deny the historical truth of the narrative, nor make it mere fable: he uses the real history of Abraham's sons as a type or illustration of a spiritual truth — distinct from the fanciful, mystery-finding allegorizing of Origen and the Alexandrian school, which all of them reject as a corruption of Scripture (Clarke prays "the spirit of silence" on such corrupters; Barnes insists the Bible be read as any other book).

On "these are the two covenants... which is Agar," the witnesses note the interpretation. Barnes and Calvin note the application: Hagar and Sarah "are (represent, stand for) the two covenants" — the one "from mount Sinai, which gendereth to bondage" (the legal covenant, bearing children unto slavery) "which is Agar (Hagar, the slave-mother)."

The lutheran Luther notes the figure: Sinai, the mountain of the law, answers to Hagar the bondmaid, bringing forth a people in bondage; the legal covenant is the slave-mother. The verse displays the allegory of the two covenants.

The witnesses agree the interpretation: Hagar and Sarah "are (represent) the two covenants" — the one "from mount Sinai, which gendereth to bondage" (the legal covenant, bearing children unto slavery), "which is Agar," the slave-mother (Barnes, Calvin, Luther). And they agree the method, carefully defined: Paul's "allegory" does not deny the history but applies the real narrative as a type — explicitly distinguished from the fanciful, mystery-finding allegorizing of Origen and the Alexandrian school, which Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, and Calvin reject as a corruption of Scripture. The verse displays the allegory of the two covenants. The verse marks the method: allegory as type. The verse displays the rejection: Origenist allegorizing. The verse displays the covenants: two. The verse displays the bondage: Sinai and Hagar. The allegory displays the legal covenant as the slave-mother. The verse displays the allegory: of real history. The verse displays the covenant: of Sinai. The verse displays the Hagar: the bondmaid. The allegory of the two covenants displays both Paul's method and its meaning: he applies the real history of Hagar and Sarah as a type (not the fanciful allegorizing of Origen, which the voices reject) to show that they represent the two covenants — Hagar and Sinai the legal covenant that bears children unto bondage. The verse displays the slave-covenant of Sinai, identified with the present Jerusalem (v.25).
25:
"For this Agar is mount Sinai in Arabia, and answereth to Jerusalem which now is, and is in bondage with her children." The brief verse notes Hagar and Sinai answering to the present Jerusalem in bondage. The grammatical voices note the identification. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's correspondence: "this Agar (Hagar) is mount Sinai in Arabia" — Hagar represents the Sinai covenant (Luther and others note "Hagar" was even an Arabian name for the mountain) — "and answereth to (corresponds with, stands in the same rank as) Jerusalem which now is."

On "Jerusalem which now is... in bondage with her children," the witnesses note the application. Barnes and Calvin note that the "present Jerusalem" — the centre of Judaism, the law, and the temple worship — "is in bondage with her children" — she and her people (the unbelieving Jews and the Judaizers) are slaves under the law, like Hagar and her son.

The lutheran Luther notes the force: the legal Jerusalem, for all her boasted privilege, is the slave-mother bearing slave-children — those who seek righteousness by the law remain in bondage. The verse displays Hagar and Sinai answering to the present Jerusalem in bondage.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's correspondence — "this Agar is mount Sinai in Arabia" (Hagar representing the Sinai covenant, even an Arabian name for the mountain) "and answereth to (corresponds with) Jerusalem which now is" (Barnes, Robertson); the "present Jerusalem" — the centre of Judaism and the law — "is in bondage with her children," the unbelieving Jews and Judaizers slaves under the law like Hagar and her son (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes the legal Jerusalem is the slave-mother bearing slave-children. The verse displays Jerusalem in bondage. The verse marks the identification: Hagar is Sinai. The verse displays the correspondence: to present Jerusalem. The verse displays the bondage: with her children. The verse displays the force: righteousness by law a slavery. The identification displays the legal Jerusalem as the slave-mother. The verse displays the Hagar: Sinai. The verse displays the Jerusalem: present. The verse displays the bondage: of her children. Hagar and Sinai answering to the present Jerusalem in bondage displays the application of the allegory: Hagar represents the Sinai covenant, which corresponds to the earthly Jerusalem — the centre of the law — and she, with her children who seek righteousness by the law, remains in bondage, the slave-mother bearing slave-children. The verse displays the bondage of legal Jerusalem, set against the free Jerusalem above (v.26).
26:
"But Jerusalem which is above is free, which is the mother of us all." The witnesses note the heavenly Jerusalem, free, the mother of believers. On "Jerusalem which is above is free," the grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the antithesis to the earthly Jerusalem: "Jerusalem which is above (the heavenly Jerusalem, the spiritual city of God, the church and commonwealth of the redeemed) is free" — not in bondage to the law, but the home of gospel liberty.

On "the mother of us all," the witnesses note the maternity. Barnes and Calvin note that this free, heavenly Jerusalem "is the mother of us all" — of all believers, Jew and Gentile; corresponding to Sarah the freewoman, she bears free children by the gospel of promise.

The eastern Cyril and lutheran Luther note the doctrine: the heavenly Jerusalem, the church of the new covenant, is the true mother of the faithful, who are born free by grace, not slaves under the law. The verse displays the heavenly Jerusalem, free, the mother of believers.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the antithesis to the earthly Jerusalem — "Jerusalem which is above (the heavenly, spiritual city of God, the church of the redeemed) is free," not in bondage to the law but the home of gospel liberty (Barnes, Robertson); she "is the mother of us all," of all believers Jew and Gentile, corresponding to Sarah the freewoman, bearing free children by the gospel of promise (Barnes, Calvin). Cyril and Luther note she is the true mother of the faithful, born free by grace. The verse displays the free heavenly Jerusalem. The verse marks the city: Jerusalem above. The verse displays the freedom: not under law. The verse displays the maternity: mother of us all. The verse displays the children: free by grace. The heavenly Jerusalem displays the free mother of the redeemed. The verse displays the city: above. The verse displays the freedom: gospel liberty. The verse displays the mother: of believers. The heavenly Jerusalem, free, the mother of believers, displays the glorious antithesis to legal bondage: over against the earthly Jerusalem in slavery stands the Jerusalem above — the spiritual city of God, free from the law — who, like Sarah, is the mother of all believers, bearing free children by the gospel of promise. The verse displays the free mother of the faithful, confirmed by prophecy (v.27).
27:
"For it is written, Rejoice, thou barren that bearest not; break forth and cry, thou that travailest not: for the desolate hath many more children than she which hath an husband." The brief verse notes the prophecy of the barren woman's many children. The grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes Isaiah 54:1: "Rejoice, thou barren that bearest not... for the desolate hath many more children than she which hath an husband" — a prophecy of the abundant offspring of the once-barren.

On the application, the witnesses note the fulfilment. Barnes and Calvin note Paul applies the prophecy to Sarah and the heavenly Jerusalem: the "barren" and "desolate" (Sarah, long childless; the church of the Gentiles, once without the promises) is to rejoice, for she shall have "many more children" than the married woman (the synagogue, the legal Jerusalem) — the free mother's offspring exceeding the bond.

The lutheran Luther notes the gospel triumph: the church of grace, once seemingly barren and forsaken, becomes fruitful beyond the legal synagogue, filling the world with free children of promise. The verse displays the prophecy of the barren woman's many children.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul quotes Isaiah 54:1 — "Rejoice, thou barren... for the desolate hath many more children than she which hath an husband," a prophecy of abundant offspring (Barnes, Robertson); applied to Sarah and the heavenly Jerusalem, the once-barren and desolate (the church of the Gentiles) having more children than the legal synagogue (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes the church of grace becomes fruitful beyond the synagogue. The verse displays the barren woman's children. The verse marks the citation: Isaiah 54. The verse displays the barren: rejoicing. The verse displays the abundance: more children. The verse displays the application: the free mother. The prophecy displays the once-barren church made fruitful. The verse displays the barren: bearing many. The verse displays the desolate: fruitful. The verse displays the children: of promise. The prophecy of the barren woman's many children displays the scriptural confirmation of the free mother's fruitfulness: as Isaiah foretold, the once-barren and desolate — Sarah, the heavenly Jerusalem, the church of grace — is to rejoice, for her children of promise far exceed those of the legal synagogue. The verse displays the fruitfulness of the free mother, whose children are the heirs (v.28).
28:
"Now we, brethren, as Isaac was, are the children of promise." The brief verse notes believers as children of promise like Isaac. The grammatical voices note the identity. Barnes and Robertson note Paul applies the allegory directly: "now we, brethren, as Isaac was, are the children of promise" — believers correspond to Isaac, born not by the flesh but by God's promise; they are the free, promised offspring.

On "children of promise," the witnesses note the ground. Barnes and Henry note that believers are children "of promise" — born by the power of God's promise and grace (like Isaac, born beyond nature), not by works of the law; their sonship rests on grace.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the assurance: as Isaac, the son of promise, was the true heir, so believers, the children of promise, are the true heirs of the inheritance — free, not slaves. The verse displays believers as children of promise like Isaac.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul applies the allegory directly — "now we, brethren, as Isaac was, are the children of promise," believers corresponding to Isaac, born by God's promise not the flesh, the free promised offspring (Barnes, Robertson); born by the power of God's promise and grace, not works of the law (Barnes, Henry). Wesley notes that as Isaac was the true heir, so believers are the true heirs, free not slaves. The verse displays children of promise. The verse marks the identity: as Isaac was. The verse displays the ground: of promise. The verse displays the birth: by grace. The verse displays the assurance: true heirs. The identity displays believers as the free, promised offspring. The verse displays the children: of promise. The verse displays the Isaac: their type. The verse displays the heirs: true. Believers as children of promise like Isaac display the application of the allegory to the faithful: as Isaac was born not by the flesh but by God's promise, so believers are the children of promise — born by grace, free, and the true heirs of the inheritance. The verse displays the believer's promised sonship, though persecuted by the children of the flesh (v.29).
29:
"But as then he that was born after the flesh persecuted him that was born after the Spirit, even so it is now." The brief verse notes the child of the flesh persecuting the child of the Spirit, then and now. The grammatical voices note the historical parallel. Barnes and Robertson note Paul observes that "then" (in Abraham's house) "he that was born after the flesh (Ishmael) persecuted (mocked, harassed, Genesis 21:9) him that was born after the Spirit (Isaac)" — the elder, fleshly son persecuting the younger, promised son.

On "even so it is now," the witnesses note the application. Barnes and Calvin note the parallel to Paul's day: "even so it is now" — the children of the flesh (the unbelieving Jews and Judaizers, who trust the law) persecute the children of the Spirit (the believers, born of promise); the pattern repeats.

The lutheran Luther notes the comfort: it has ever been the lot of the children of promise to be persecuted by the children of the law; the believer should not be dismayed but expect such opposition. The verse displays the child of the flesh persecuting the child of the Spirit.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "then" (in Abraham's house) "he that was born after the flesh (Ishmael) persecuted (mocked, Genesis 21:9) him that was born after the Spirit (Isaac)" (Barnes, Robertson); "even so it is now," the children of the flesh (unbelieving Jews and Judaizers) persecuting the children of the Spirit (believers born of promise), the pattern repeating (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes it is ever the lot of the promised children to be persecuted by the legal. The verse displays the persecution. The verse marks the parallel: then Ishmael persecuted Isaac. The verse displays the application: even so now. The verse displays the persecutors: children of the flesh. The verse displays the persecuted: children of the Spirit. The persecution displays the fleshly harassing the promised. The verse displays the flesh: persecuting. The verse displays the Spirit: persecuted. The verse displays the pattern: repeating. The child of the flesh persecuting the child of the Spirit, then and now, displays the recurring pattern of opposition: as Ishmael mocked Isaac, so in Paul's day the children of the law persecute the children of promise — the believer must expect such hostility, for it has ever been the lot of the free children of grace. The verse displays the persecution of the promised, answered by the Scripture's verdict (v.30).
30:
"Nevertheless what saith the scripture? Cast out the bondwoman and her son: for the son of the bondwoman shall not be heir with the son of the freewoman." The witnesses note the Scripture's verdict to cast out the bondwoman and her son. On "what saith the scripture? Cast out the bondwoman and her son," the grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul appeals to Scripture's decisive word (Genesis 21:10, Sarah's demand, ratified by God): "cast out (expel, send away) the bondwoman (Hagar) and her son (Ishmael)" — the slave and her child are to be put out of the house.

On "the son of the bondwoman shall not be heir with the son of the freewoman," the witnesses note the disinheritance. Barnes and Calvin note the ground: the slave's son "shall not be heir with" the free son — the two cannot share the inheritance; the child of bondage is excluded from the inheritance reserved for the child of promise.

On the application, the lutheran Luther notes the force: the covenant of law and its children (the Judaizers, those who seek righteousness by works) must be cast out; they cannot inherit with the children of grace. The verse displays the Scripture's verdict to cast out the bondwoman and her son.

The witnesses agree: Paul appeals to Scripture's decisive word (Genesis 21:10, Sarah's demand ratified by God) — "cast out the bondwoman (Hagar) and her son (Ishmael)," the slave and her child put out of the house (Barnes, Robertson); the ground being that the slave's son "shall not be heir with" the free son, excluded from the inheritance reserved for the child of promise (Barnes, Calvin); so the covenant of law and its children must be cast out, unable to inherit with the children of grace (Luther). The verse displays the verdict to cast out. The verse marks the citation: cast out the bondwoman. The verse displays the disinheritance: not heir together. The verse displays the exclusion: of the bondchild. The verse displays the application: law cast out. The verdict displays the children of bondage excluded from the inheritance. The verse displays the casting out: of Hagar. The verse displays the heir: only the free son. The verse displays the law: excluded. The Scripture's verdict to cast out the bondwoman and her son displays the decisive word against legalism: as God ratified the expulsion of Hagar and Ishmael, so the covenant of law and those who seek righteousness by it cannot share the inheritance with the children of promise — the bond and the free cannot be heirs together. The verse displays the disinheriting of the law's children, concluding the believer's freedom (v.31).
31:
"So then, brethren, we are not children of the bondwoman, but of the free." The brief verse notes the conclusion: believers are children of the free, not the bond. The grammatical voices note the summing. Barnes and Robertson note Paul draws the conclusion ("so then") of the whole allegory: "we are not children of the bondwoman (Hagar, the law, bondage) but of the free (Sarah, the gospel, liberty)" — believers belong to the free mother.

On "of the free," the witnesses note the liberty. Barnes and Calvin note the triumphant result: believers are free children, heirs of the promise and of gospel freedom, not slaves under the law; their standing is that of Isaac, the free son and heir.

The lutheran Luther notes the doctrine: this is the heart of the epistle — the believer is free from the law as a means of righteousness, a child of the free covenant of grace, and must not return to bondage. The verse displays the conclusion: believers are children of the free.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul concludes the allegory — "we are not children of the bondwoman (Hagar, the law, bondage) but of the free (Sarah, the gospel, liberty)," believers belonging to the free mother (Barnes, Robertson); the triumphant result that believers are free children, heirs of promise and gospel freedom, not slaves, their standing that of Isaac (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes this is the heart of the epistle — the believer free from the law as a means of righteousness. The verse displays children of the free. The verse marks the conclusion: not of the bondwoman. The verse displays the standing: of the free. The verse displays the liberty: gospel freedom. The verse displays the doctrine: free from the law. The conclusion displays believers as the free children of grace. The verse displays the bondwoman: not their mother. The verse displays the free: their mother. The verse displays the freedom: theirs. The conclusion that believers are children of the free, not the bond, displays the triumphant summary of the whole allegory: believers belong not to Hagar and the law's bondage but to Sarah and the gospel's liberty — free children, heirs of the promise like Isaac, who must not return to the yoke of slavery. The verse displays the believer's gospel freedom, the crown of the chapter and the ground of the exhortation to stand fast (ch. 5).
